topic: Two people discover they've been working on identical projects
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias traced the faint, almost invisible seam on the casing of the prototype. His breath hitched, a shallow, quick intake of air that did little to steady the tremor in his hand. He leaned closer to the device, his eyes darting from the intricate circuitry to the perfectly rendered schematic spread across the workbench. It was the same configuration. Every wire, every soldered joint, every notation in the logbook matched his own work, down to the minute imperfections he’d introduced hours ago. A cold knot tightened in his stomach, pulling at his chest. He stood up abruptly, the chair scraping harshly against the concrete floor. He needed to see if anyone else was in the lab. The silence of the otherwise deserted workshop seemed to amplify the frantic thumping in his ears.

[story 2]

"This is impossible," Sarah murmured, her voice tight, barely above a whisper. She stared at the finished sculpture—the swirling obsidian form she had labored over for weeks. Then she looked at the folder open beside it, the file labeled 'Project Chimera,' and the identical design blueprints. Her fingers began to drum a rapid, uneven rhythm against the metal surface of the desk. A flush crept up her neck, hot and undeniable. She felt a sudden, profound sense of dislocation, as if the ground beneath her feet had shifted. She slowly reached out and touched the smooth, cool surface of the sculpture, her gaze locked on the reflection of her own anxious face in the polished black material.

[story 3]

I found the schematics tucked beneath the power supply. They were flawless. Too flawless. My hands were slick with sweat as I ran a finger over the diagram detailing the energy conduit layout. Then I saw the handwritten notes beside the diagram—my notes. The specific notations about the resonance frequency, the unusual alloy mixture—it was all mine. A strange hollowness expanded in my chest, making it hard to draw a full breath. I backed away from the table, my heel catching on a loose cable. I didn't move toward the door; I just stood there, frozen, listening to the distant hum of the ventilation system, certain that the air itself felt thick and suffocating.

I found the schematics tucked beneath the power supply. They were flawless. Too flawless. My hands were slick with sweat as I ran a finger over the diagram detailing the energy conduit layout. Then I saw the handwritten notes beside the diagram—my notes. The specific notations about the resonance frequency, the unusual alloy mixture—it was all mine. A strange hollowness expanded in my chest, making it hard to draw a full breath. I backed away from the table, my heel catching on a loose cable. I didn't move toward the door; I just stood there, frozen, listening to the distant hum of the ventilation system, certain that the air itself felt thick and suffocating.
